The craft beer selection is well-chosen, and the outdoor area captures the Whitianga sun. It's the perfect casual dinner after a day at the beach or Hot Water Beach. The portions are generous and the prices are reasonable for a tourist town.

reviews.summary: The smoked meats are surprisingly authentic. Good craft beer selection. Casual and unpretentious — perfect for the Coromandel vibe.

pois.name: Cathedral Cove Marine Reserve
pois.short: Crystal-clear water, volcanic reefs, and abundant fish life in the Coromandel's premier marine reserve.
pois.commentary: Te Whanganui-A-Hei Marine Reserve protects 840 hectares of coast around Cathedral Cove. Snorkelling reveals massive snapper, crayfish, kina, and colourful reef fish. Gemstone Bay has an underwater snorkel trail with interpretive signs.

reviews.summary: Outstanding snorkelling. Gemstone Bay underwater trail is excellent. Water clearest in summer. Busy in January.

pois.name: Kuaotunu Kiwi Sanctuary
pois.short: Community-led kiwi conservation — North Island brown kiwi are returning to the bush above Kuaotunu.
pois.commentary: The Kuaotunu Bird Rescue and Conservation Trust has been running intensive predator control since 2005. Kiwi numbers are increasing and you can hear them calling at night along Blackjack Road. The trust runs occasional guided listening walks.

reviews.summary: Exciting to hear kiwi in the wild. Best in winter/spring evenings. Support the local trust.

pois.name: Whangapoua Beach — NZ Dotterel Colony
pois.short: One of the Coromandel's most important NZ dotterel nesting beaches — protected by volunteer wardens.
pois.commentary: Whangapoua hosts one of the Hauraki's largest NZ dotterel breeding populations. DOC and volunteer wardens protect nests with predator traps and temporary fencing during the Sept-Feb breeding season. The beach is stunning and relatively uncrowded.

reviews.summary: Beautiful beach with real conservation happening. Keep dogs on leads. The dotterels are surprisingly well-camouflaged.

pois.name: Ōpito Bay — Marine Life Snorkelling
pois.short: Sheltered bay with excellent reef snorkelling — parore, snapper, and eagle rays in warm, clear water.
pois.commentary: Ōpito Bay is one of the Coromandel's most sheltered snorkelling spots. The rocky reef on the south side of the bay hosts parore, snapper, triplefin, and occasional eagle rays. The water is warm by NZ standards and usually calm.

reviews.summary: Great snorkelling in calm, warm water. Less crowded than Cathedral Cove. Best at high tide.

pois.name: Thames Coast — Pūkorokoro Miranda
pois.short: One of NZ's most important shorebird sites — bar-tailed godwit, royal spoonbill, and wrybill.
pois.commentary: The Firth of Thames mudflats at Miranda/Pūkorokoro are internationally significant for migratory wading birds. The Robert Findlay Wildlife Reserve and Stilt Ponds offer excellent viewing. The Miranda Shorebird Centre provides binoculars and expert advice.

reviews.summary: World-class birding. The shorebird centre is excellent. Low tide is essential. Spring and autumn migration peaks.

pois.name: The Pinnacles — Long-Tailed Bat Habitat
pois.short: The Kauaeranga Valley forests are home to the endangered long-tailed bat — listen for echolocation clicks at dusk.
pois.commentary: The Kauaeranga Valley's mature podocarp forests provide habitat for the endangered long-tailed bat (pekapeka). As you hike to The Pinnacles, dusk brings the chance to see bats flitting along forest edges. DOC bat monitoring stations operate in the valley.

reviews.summary: Classic Coromandel tramp. Bat sightings are a bonus — they're hard to spot but bat detectors help.

pois.name: Wentworth Valley — Bush Bird Walk
pois.short: Easy bush walk through pest-controlled forest — North Island robin, tomtit, and whiteheads are common.
pois.commentary: The Wentworth Valley near Whangamata has benefited from years of community pest control. The loop walk passes through beautiful bush where North Island robin, tomtit, and whiteheads are regularly seen. The waterfall at the end is a bonus.

reviews.summary: Lovely walk with good birding. Robins are very tame. The waterfall is worth the walk alone.

pois.name: Mahakirau Forest Estate — Kiwi Sanctuary
pois.short: Private forest estate with intensive kiwi protection — one of the Coromandel's kiwi strongholds.
pois.commentary: Mahakirau Forest Estate is a private conservation trust running intensive predator control across 1,000 hectares of Coromandel bush. Kiwi numbers have increased significantly, and the estate is now a source population for kiwi translocations to other sites.

reviews.summary: Important conservation work. Limited public access but the trust welcomes supporters and volunteers.

pois.name: Moehau Range — Northern Kiwi Stronghold
pois.short: DOC-managed kiwi sanctuary in the rugged northern Coromandel — one of the peninsula's most important kiwi populations.
pois.commentary: The Moehau Range at the northern tip of the Coromandel is home to a significant population of North Island brown kiwi. DOC runs an intensive stoat trapping programme here to protect kiwi chicks. The tramping tracks are challenging but the bush is spectacular.

reviews.summary: Serious tramping territory. Bush is dense and rugged. The kiwi population here is nationally important.

pois.name: New Chums Beach — Coastal Wildlife
pois.short: Pristine undeveloped beach with NZ dotterel, variable oystercatcher, and reef fish in the rock pools.
pois.commentary: New Chums Beach is regularly voted one of NZ's most beautiful beaches. Its remoteness (30-minute walk through bush) means it's also good for coastal wildlife — NZ dotterel nest here, variable oystercatchers patrol the tide line, and the rock pools are rich with marine life.

reviews.summary: Stunning beach. The walk in keeps crowds down. Look for dotterel and oystercatchers on the sand.

pois.name: Mercury Bay Museum Whitianga
pois.short: Charming community museum telling the stories of Mercury Bay's maritime, kauri, and pioneering history.
pois.commentary: Mercury Bay Museum in Whitianga is a lovingly curated community museum telling the rich stories of this historic coastal area. Mercury Bay is named after Captain Cook's 1769 observation of the transit of Mercury from here, and the museum covers this significant event alongside the earlier Polynesian voyaging history, the kauri logging and gum-digging era, and maritime heritage. Displays include a replica of Cook's astronomical equipment, historic photographs, Māori taonga, and maritime artefacts. The museum is housed in a heritage building and staffed by passionate local volunteers who bring the stories to life. It is a small museum but punches well above its weight in the quality of its displays and storytelling. A visit provides excellent context for exploring the wider Mercury Bay area, including Cathedral Cove and Hot Water Beach. Open daily during summer, reduced hours in winter. Gold coin donation appreciated.

reviews.summary: A delightful small museum run by wonderful volunteers. Really helps you understand the history of the Coromandel area. The Cook connection is fascinating and well presented.

pois.name: Kuaotunu Beach
pois.short: Unspoilt golden sand beach in a relaxed Coromandel settlement, perfect for swimming and kayaking.
pois.commentary: Kuaotunu Beach is one of the Coromandel Peninsula's most appealing and unspoilt beaches, a lovely crescent of golden sand in a quiet holiday settlement north of Whitianga. The beach is sheltered and safe for swimming, with warm, clear water in summer that makes it popular with families. The small community maintains a relaxed, uncommercialised atmosphere — there is a charming general store and the acclaimed Luke's Kitchen café, but otherwise it is beach, bush, and birdsong. The beach faces north, catching sun all day, and the backdrop of native bush-clad hills adds to the beauty. Kayaking and stand-up paddleboarding are popular in the calm conditions. The Black Jack Road scenic drive from Kuaotunu to Whitianga is a winding coastal route with spectacular views. Kuaotunu also provides access to some beautiful coastal walks. Summer holiday period gets busy but never crowded. Limited facilities, so bring supplies.

reviews.summary: One of the Coromandel's best-kept secrets — gorgeous, uncrowded beach with crystal-clear water. Luke's Kitchen does amazing food. The perfect place to slow down and unwind.

pois.name: Whangapoua Beach
pois.short: Gateway to New Chums Beach, with a wild and beautiful main beach backed by native bush.
pois.commentary: Whangapoua Beach on the Coromandel's eastern coast is a stunning white-sand beach renowned as the access point for New Chums Beach, regularly voted one of the world's best undeveloped beaches. The main Whangapoua Beach itself is a beautiful, wide stretch of sand framed by native bush and headlands, excellent for swimming and bodyboarding. To reach New Chums, walk around the northern headland at low tide or take the track over the hill — the 30-minute walk is rewarded with a pristine, road-free beach that feels truly wild. Whangapoua is a small holiday community with no shops, adding to its remote, unspoilt character. The beach is accessed via a winding gravel road from Kuaotunu. Swimming conditions are generally good but the beach is not patrolled, so take care with currents. Visit midweek or off-season for solitude. Bring everything you need including water and sun protection.

reviews.summary: Worth the winding gravel road for two incredible beaches. New Chums over the headland is otherworldly. Main beach is gorgeous too. Bring supplies as there's nothing here but nature.

pois.name: Matarangi Beach
pois.short: Long golden sand beach in an upmarket Coromandel resort community with safe swimming and ocean views.
pois.commentary: Matarangi Beach stretches for several kilometres along the northern Coromandel coast, a broad expanse of golden sand backed by an upmarket resort community and golf course. The beach offers safe swimming conditions with relatively gentle surf, making it popular with families. The Dunes Golf Course provides a links-style golfing experience with ocean views. Matarangi has grown from a small holiday spot into one of the Coromandel's premier resort communities, with a café, restaurant, and well-maintained facilities. The beach is less well-known than neighbouring Whitianga or Hot Water Beach, meaning it remains relatively uncrowded even in peak summer. Walking the full length of the beach at low tide is a wonderful experience. The area also offers access to the Rings Beach walk and other coastal tracks. A relaxed, family-oriented destination that captures the best of the Coromandel's beach lifestyle without the crowds.

reviews.summary: A beautiful, spacious beach that never feels too busy. Great for families with safe swimming and plenty of room. The golf course is a bonus and the café does good food.

pois.name: Driving Creek Railway
pois.short: Quirky narrow-gauge railway climbing through native bush and art installations to a hilltop viewpoint.
pois.commentary: Driving Creek Railway is the creation of potter and conservationist Barry Brickell, who spent decades building this narrow-gauge mountain railway through regenerating native kauri forest on his property near Coromandel Town. The one-hour return journey climbs 165 metres through tunnels, across bridges, and around spirals, passing Brickell's ceramic sculptures and art installations integrated into the bush. The terminus at the Eyefull Tower provides panoramic views over Coromandel Harbour and the Hauraki Gulf. The railway was built primarily to carry clay and firewood for Brickell's pottery kiln, but became a beloved tourist attraction. The surrounding land is a conservation project with extensive native tree planting. Multiple departures run daily and booking is recommended, especially during summer. The pottery and gallery at the base are worth browsing.

reviews.summary: A truly unique New Zealand experience that delights visitors of all ages. The combination of engineering ingenuity, art, and conservation is inspiring. The hilltop views are stunning on a clear day.

pois.name: The Waterworks
pois.short: Eccentric water-powered theme park in the bush with kinetic sculptures and interactive water contraptions.
pois.commentary: The Waterworks is a delightfully eccentric interactive park hidden in native bush north of Coromandel Town. Created from recycled materials, the park features dozens of water-powered contraptions, kinetic sculptures, musical instruments, and hands-on exhibits spread along bush tracks beside a stream. Highlights include water cannons, a flying fox, pedal-powered watercraft on a small lake, and a water-powered clock. The park embodies Kiwi ingenuity and DIY spirit, appealing equally to children who love getting wet and adults who appreciate the inventive engineering. Allow at least two hours and bring a change of clothes for children. The bush setting is beautiful, with swimming holes in the stream during summer. Located 7km north of Coromandel Town along the 309 Road, a scenic gravel route across the peninsula.

reviews.summary: A wonderfully quirky and uniquely Kiwi attraction. Kids absolutely love the interactive water play while parents appreciate the creative engineering. The bush setting adds to the charm.

pois.name: Coromandel Stamper Battery
pois.short: Restored 19th-century gold stamper battery and water wheel near Coromandel Town, a working heritage attraction.
pois.commentary: The Coromandel Stamper Battery is a restored gold-processing plant dating from the 1890s gold-mining era, located a short drive from Coromandel Town along the 309 Road. The battery features a massive working water wheel that once powered the stamps used to crush gold-bearing quartz rock. Guided tours demonstrate the stamping process, explaining how raw ore was crushed to extract gold, and tell the stories of the miners who worked the Coromandel goldfields. The site includes the original machinery, settling tables, and processing equipment in their bush setting. The water wheel, powered by a diverted creek, is particularly impressive and still operational. The 309 Road itself is a scenic gravel route across the peninsula, connecting the eastern and western coastlines. Combine with nearby attractions including The Waterworks and Castle Rock walk for a full day on the 309 Road. A hands-on heritage experience that brings gold-rush history to life.

reviews.summary: A fascinating working heritage site that brings gold-mining history to life. The water wheel is impressive and the guided explanations are engaging. A good stop on the scenic 309 Road.

pois.name: Hot Water Beach
pois.short: Unique beach where hot thermal water rises through the sand, allowing you to dig your own hot pool at low tide.
pois.commentary: Hot Water Beach is one of New Zealand's most unique natural experiences, where geothermal springs rise through the sand on a stretch of Coromandel coastline. At low tide, visitors dig holes in the sand with spades (available for hire from nearby shops) to create personal hot pools that fill with naturally heated water. The thermal area is confined to a small section of the beach, marked by steam rising from the sand, and temperatures can exceed 64 degrees Celsius, so mixing with cold seawater is essential to achieve a comfortable bathing temperature. The experience works best in the two hours either side of low tide. The beach itself is beautiful, backed by headlands and popular for surfing. The thermal springs are believed to be connected to the same volcanic system that feeds the Coromandel's other geothermal features. Located on the eastern Coromandel coast between Tairua and Whitianga, it is a must-visit on any Coromandel road trip. Can get very busy in peak summer.

reviews.summary: A truly unique natural experience that delights visitors of all ages. Digging your own hot pool on a beach is pure joy. Check tide times carefully and arrive early in peak season to secure a good spot.

pois.name: Cathedral Cove
pois.short: Iconic natural sea arch on a pristine white sand beach, reached by a scenic coastal walk through native bush.
pois.commentary: Cathedral Cove (Te Whanganui-A-Hei) is the Coromandel Peninsula's most iconic landmark, a dramatic natural sea arch framing a white sand beach between sculpted rock walls. The cove is reached by a scenic 45-minute walk from the car park at Hahei, descending through native bush with views across Mercury Bay to volcanic islands. The trail passes several smaller bays and lookout points before arriving at the cove, where the massive arch connects two beaches. The rock formations are sculpted from ancient volcanic ash (ignimbrite), and the clear turquoise water is excellent for swimming and snorkelling. The area is a marine reserve, rich in marine life. Water taxi and kayak access is available from Hahei for those who prefer not to walk. The cove featured in the Chronicles of Narnia: Prince Caspian. A new shuttle bus service from Hahei to the trailhead eases parking pressure during peak summer. Early morning visits offer the best light and fewer people.

reviews.summary: One of New Zealand's most beautiful natural features. The walk to the cove is scenic and the arch itself is breathtaking. Visit early morning or late afternoon for the best experience.

pois.name: Thames Goldmine Experience
pois.short: Underground gold mine tour and museum in Thames, the gateway town to the Coromandel Peninsula.
pois.commentary: The Thames Goldmine Experience offers a guided underground tour through a historic gold mine in Thames, the former gold-mining capital of New Zealand. In its 1870s heyday, Thames had a population of 18,000 and was one of the largest towns in the country, fuelled by the wealth extracted from the quartz reefs beneath the surrounding hills. The tour takes visitors underground into actual mine tunnels where the working conditions, mining techniques, and geological formations are explained by knowledgeable guides. Above ground, the museum displays mining equipment, gold specimens, and photographs from the boom years. The experience provides valuable context for understanding the Coromandel's gold-mining heritage, which shaped the peninsula's settlement patterns and landscape. Thames itself retains heritage character in its main street buildings. Located at the southern gateway to the Coromandel Peninsula, the goldmine makes an interesting stop before heading up the coast.

reviews.summary: An engaging underground experience that brings gold-mining history to life. The guides are informative and the tunnel walk is atmospheric. A good introduction to the Coromandel's mining heritage.

pois.name: Luke's Kitchen Kuaotunu
pois.short: Celebrated restaurant in a surf club setting overlooking Kuaotunu Beach.
pois.commentary: Luke's Kitchen has become one of the Coromandel Peninsula's most talked-about dining destinations, located in the Kuaotunu Surf Life Saving Club with stunning views across the beach. Chef Luke Simon creates a menu focused on fresh local seafood and seasonal produce, with a relaxed atmosphere that matches the beach setting. The wine list features New Zealand producers and the cocktail menu uses local ingredients. Booking is essential during summer as the restaurant has limited seating and a devoted following. The setting is unbeatable on a warm evening, with the sun setting over the beach as you dine. Open seasonally with reduced winter hours. The drive to Kuaotunu from Whitianga takes about twenty minutes through scenic bush.

reviews.summary: Outstanding food in an unbeatable beach setting. Booking is essential. One of the best dining experiences on the Coromandel.

pois.name: Karangahake Gorge Historic Walkway
pois.short: Walk through mining tunnels, over bridges, and past ruins in a dramatic river gorge.
pois.commentary: Karangahake Gorge is where the Ōhinemuri River cuts through the Kaimai Range, and where gold miners tunnelled from the 1870s.

reviews.summary: One of NZ's best heritage walks. The tunnels are thrilling and the gorge is beautiful.

pois.name: Victoria Battery, Waikino
pois.short: Largest gold-stamping battery in NZ when built 1897 — underground tours and heritage tramway.
pois.commentary: The Victoria Battery at Waikino was the largest gold-stamping battery in NZ when built in 1897.

reviews.summary: Impressive industrial heritage. Underground tours are fascinating. Heritage tramway is a nice touch.

pois.name: Thames School of Mines & Treasury
pois.short: Historic mining school (1886-1954) now housing mining artifacts and mineralogical museum.
pois.commentary: The Thames School of Mines operated from 1886 to 1954 and now houses a museum with mining artifacts and a remarkable mineralogical collection.

reviews.summary: Excellent mining heritage museum with knowledgeable volunteers. The mineral collection is impressive.

pois.name: Coromandel Town Heritage Walk
pois.short: Charming gold-rush era town with heritage buildings, art galleries, and artisan studios.
pois.commentary: Coromandel Town retains the character of a gold-rush era settlement with colonial heritage buildings.

reviews.summary: A charming small town with genuine heritage character. The art galleries are worth browsing.

pois.name: Ōtama Beach
pois.short: A stunning crescent of white sand far less crowded than Cathedral Cove or Hot Water Beach.
pois.commentary: Ōtama Beach is a stunning crescent of white sand on the eastern Coromandel coast.

reviews.summary: A beautiful quiet beach — the Coromandel's best-kept secret. Safe swimming and lovely white sand.

pois.name: Wentworth Valley Walk & Waterfall
pois.short: Beautiful bush walk to a cascading waterfall with a deep swimming hole surrounded by ferns.
pois.commentary: The Wentworth Valley Walk near Whangamatā follows a stream through native bush to a cascading waterfall.

reviews.summary: Lovely bush walk with a rewarding waterfall at the end. The swimming hole is refreshing.

pois.name: Thames Goldmine Experience
pois.short: Go underground in a real mine, learn about stamper batteries, and try gold panning.
pois.commentary: The Thames Goldmine Experience takes visitors underground into a real gold mine from the 1860s gold rush era.

reviews.summary: Going underground in a real mine is exciting. Gold panning is fun for all ages.

pois.name: Coroglen Farmers Market & Pub
pois.short: Popular Saturday market and legendary rural pub with live music — a Coromandel institution.
pois.commentary: The Coroglen Farmers Market runs every Saturday morning during summer. The neighbouring Coroglen Tavern hosts legendary live music events.

reviews.summary: A great Saturday morning market with genuine local produce. The Coroglen Tavern gigs are legendary.

pois.name: The 309 Road — Waiau Kauri Grove & Waterworks
pois.short: Winding unsealed road with ancient kauri trees and the bizarre Waterworks.
pois.commentary: The 309 Road crosses the Coromandel Range with two remarkable stops: the Waiau Kauri Grove and the quirky Waterworks.

reviews.summary: The ancient kauri trees are awe-inspiring. The Waterworks is brilliantly quirky. Allow more time than you think.

pois.name: Te Whanganui-A-Hei Marine Reserve
pois.short: Marine reserve surrounding Cathedral Cove — exceptional snorkelling, kayaking, and marine life.
pois.commentary: Te Whanganui-A-Hei Marine Reserve protects 840 hectares of coastal marine environment around Cathedral Cove.

reviews.summary: The snorkelling is exceptional — huge snapper everywhere. Kayaking through the sea caves is magical.

pois.name: Waiau Kauri Grove
pois.short: A grove of ancient kauri trees saved from the loggers by local residents — a 30-minute loop walk through cathedral-like forest.
pois.commentary: Down the gravel 309 Road between Coromandel Town and Whitianga, a short track leads into a grove of mature kauri — some over 1,000 years old — that survived 19th-century logging because locals fought to protect them. The trees aren’t as large as their Northland cousins (Tāne Mahuta and friends), but the grove is atmospheric and uncrowded. The boardwalk loop takes about 30 minutes. Combine with the nearby Waiau Waterfall, a 10-minute walk to a pretty cascade.

reviews.summary: Visitors love the peaceful atmosphere and the chance to see mature kauri without the crowds of Northland. The short walk makes it accessible for all fitness levels. Some note the 309 Road is rough gravel.

pois.name: Homunga Bay
pois.short: A hidden cove reached by a 45-minute bush track — one of the Coromandel’s best-kept secrets.
pois.commentary: Homunga Bay sits between Cathedral Cove and Hahei but sees a fraction of the visitors because the only access is a 45-minute tramping track through coastal bush. The reward is a crescent of golden sand, usually empty, with clear water and pohutukawa shade. The track starts near the Cathedral Cove car park but heads north instead of south. Not suitable for young children due to the steep descent. Pack water and snacks — there’s nothing here but you and the sea.

reviews.summary: Those who make the effort describe it as magical — a private beach without the Cathedral Cove crowds. The track is steep in places and can be muddy after rain.

pois.name: Waihi Gold Discovery Centre
pois.short: Interactive museum telling the story of the Martha Mine — New Zealand’s richest gold and silver mine, still operating since 1878.
pois.commentary: The Gold Discovery Centre sits right beside the Martha Mine pit in Waihi, and tells the story of a mine that has produced over 7 million ounces of gold since 1878. Interactive displays cover everything from the hard-rock mining techniques of the Victorian era to today’s open-pit and underground operations. You can pan for gold, explore a replica mine tunnel, and take a guided tour of the actual mine rim. The Martha Mine is one of the few working mines in the world that sits in the middle of a town.

reviews.summary: Families and history buffs enjoy the interactive exhibits. The mine rim walk with views into the enormous pit is a highlight. Some feel it could be updated but the gold panning is fun for kids.

pois.name: Coromandel Coastal Walk
pois.short: A spectacular private coastal walk across remote northern Coromandel farmland and bush — 7 hours through some of the peninsula’s wildest country.
pois.commentary: This privately operated walk traverses the wild northern tip of the Coromandel Peninsula, crossing farmland, native bush and a rugged coastline that most visitors never see. The full walk takes about 7 hours one way between Stony Bay and Fletcher Bay, with water taxi options for the return. The scenery is dramatic — sheer cliffs, isolated coves, and the Hauraki Gulf stretching to Great Barrier Island. Bookings are essential as it crosses private land. Pack lunch and plenty of water.

reviews.summary: Walkers rave about the wild, remote scenery and the sense of having the coast to yourself. The track is well-maintained but fitness is required. Water taxi pickup makes logistics easier.

pois.name: Mercury Bay Art Escape
pois.short: An annual open-studios art trail across Mercury Bay — painters, sculptors, ceramicists and jewellers open their doors to visitors.
pois.commentary: Each January, over 40 artists around Whitianga, Hahei, Cooks Beach and Hot Water Beach open their studios to the public for the Mercury Bay Art Escape. You can watch artists at work, buy directly, and discover creative spaces tucked into coastal bush and farmland. Even outside the festival, several studios remain open year-round. The event has been running since 2010 and has grown into one of the North Island’s best regional art trails.

reviews.summary: Art lovers praise the quality and variety of work on display. The chance to meet artists in their own studios is a highlight. Some wish the event ran longer than the annual window.

pois.name: The Pinnacles Sunrise Hike
pois.short: An overnight hike to the Kauaeranga Valley summit for sunrise — 360-degree views over the Coromandel and beyond.
pois.commentary: The Pinnacles Track in the Kauaeranga Valley is the Coromandel’s signature overnight hike. The track climbs through regenerating kauri forest (logged heavily in the 19th century) to the Pinnacles Hut, where you stay overnight and climb to the summit at dawn for a sunrise view that takes in the Coromandel Peninsula, Bay of Plenty, Hauraki Gulf and on clear days, the volcanic plateau. The hut (DOC, bookings required) sleeps 80. The track is well-formed but steep in sections — 750 m elevation gain over 7 km. Allow 3–4 hours up.

reviews.summary: The sunrise from the summit is consistently described as unforgettable. The hut is comfortable. The kauri dam remnants along the track add historical interest. Book the hut well in advance for weekends.

pois.name: Pacific Coast Highway (Coromandel Section)
pois.short: The winding coastal road around the Coromandel Peninsula — beaches, bush and pohutukawa-lined bays.
pois.commentary: The road is narrow and winding in places but the scenery is spectacular: pohutukawa-lined bays, golden beaches, bush-clad ridges and ocean views.

reviews.summary: A beautiful drive that rewards slow travel.

pois.name: Driving Creek Railway and Potteries
pois.short: A unique 15-inch-gauge bush railway hand-built by potter Barry Brickell on his own land — climbing 165 metres through regenerating native forest to the EyeFull Tower viewpoint.
pois.commentary: Driving Creek Railway is one of New Zealand's most eccentric and beloved attractions — a passion project that became a conservation triumph. Brickell began building in 1975 on 22 hectares of once-degraded farmland, originally to transport clay and pine fuel to his pottery kilns. Over 40 years the railway expanded, the land was replanted in native species, and what was a weed-covered hillside became a thriving kauri and podocarp forest supporting kiwi, kōkako and other native wildlife. The train crosses ten bridges, passes through three tunnels and terminates at the EyeFull Tower summit with views across the Hauraki Gulf. The potteries below produce Barry's distinctive ceramic art. Engineering New Zealand lists the railway as a Heritage Engineering Record.

reviews.summary: Driving Creek is one of those rare attractions that is exactly what it appears to be — an authentic, idiosyncratic, joyful creation by one committed individual. The train journey, the forest, and the summit views all deliver. One of the highlights of any Coromandel visit, including for adults who don't usually enjoy train rides.

pois.name: Coromandel School of Mines and Historical Museum
pois.short: An 1897 heritage building housing the history of the Coromandel gold rush, with original mining equipment, gold specimens, and records of the families who shaped the Peninsula.
pois.commentary: The Coromandel School of Mines was established in 1897 to train practical miners in geology, surveying, assaying and mechanical engineering during the height of the Coromandel gold rush. The building itself is heritage-listed and representative of the grand institutional architecture of the colonial boom period. The museum holds the records, equipment and personal artefacts of the families who worked the Coromandel goldfields from the 1850s onwards, as well as geological specimens including native gold in reef quartz. The Coromandel gold rush was one of New Zealand's most significant — at its peak the Peninsula hosted over 10,000 miners and was more densely populated than it is today. Staffed by knowledgeable volunteers.

reviews.summary: An intimate, well-curated local museum that brings the gold-rush era of the Coromandel Peninsula to life through genuine artefacts and family stories. The building itself is part of the attraction. Better than most small-town museums — volunteers add warmth and depth that signage alone cannot.

pois.name: Thames School of Mines and Mineralogical Museum
pois.short: The largest of New Zealand's colonial schools of mines, now housing an outstanding mineralogical collection alongside exhibits on the Thames goldfield that once produced millions of ounces of gold.
pois.commentary: Thames was the epicentre of New Zealand's most productive goldfield and the Thames School of Mines — established 1885 at the corner of Brown and Cochrane Streets — was the largest of around 30 similar institutions formed to professionalise New Zealand mining. The mineralogical museum holds one of the finest collections of New Zealand mineral specimens in the country, including extraordinary native gold samples, quartz reef sections, and specimens from now-exhausted Thames mines. Heritage New Zealand lists the building as a Category I Historic Place. The exhibits document the boom years of the 1870s when Thames was one of the largest towns in New Zealand, its population briefly exceeding Auckland's.

reviews.summary: The Thames School of Mines museum is exceptional for a small regional facility — the mineral collection is genuinely world-class and the historical narrative of the goldfield is well told. A must-stop in Thames for anyone interested in New Zealand's colonial economic history or geology.

pois.name: Whitianga Rock Historic Reserve (Ngāti Hei Pā)
pois.short: A dramatic rocky headland pā site of Ngāti Hei, visited by Captain Cook in 1769 — one of the most strategically impressive defensive fortifications in the Coromandel.
pois.commentary: An impressive pā once stood on the narrow rocky headland jutting into Whitianga Harbour, with natural rock bluffs and water providing defence on three sides while earthworks protected the landward approach. Ngāti Hei, whose ancestor Hei is said to have arrived on the Arawa waka from Hawaiki, maintained this site as a stronghold for generations. When James Cook visited in November 1769 (having been guided here from Mercury Bay), the pā had been abandoned — but Cook noted its formidable strategic position in his journal. DOC manages the reserve today, accessible via a short walk from Whitianga, with interpretation panels explaining the site's significance and Ngāti Hei history.

reviews.summary: A historically significant and visually dramatic Māori heritage site that most visitors to Whitianga walk past without knowing. The connection to Cook's 1769 visit adds a rare dual-cultural historical layer. The views over Whitianga Harbour from the headland are superb.

pois.name: Te Pare Point Historic Reserve (Hahei)
pois.short: A coastal headland preserving the remains of two Ngāti Hei pā — Hereheretaura and Hahei Pā — with panoramic Mercury Bay views accessed via a 15-minute track from Hahei Beach.
pois.commentary: Te Pare Point Historic Reserve occupies a dramatic headland at the southern end of Hahei Beach, preserving the remains of Hereheretaura Pā and Hahei Pā — two fortified Ngāti Hei settlements of significant archaeological value. The headland's natural cliffs provide extraordinary defensive position on three sides; visible earthworks including ditches, banks and scarps remain from the pre-contact period. The 15-minute track from Pā Road passes through coastal scrub to the summit, where views extend across Mercury Bay to Whitianga and east to Aldermen Islands. Interpretation panels explain the pā layout, the oral traditions of Ngāti Hei, and the cultural connections between these sites and Cathedral Cove Marine Reserve directly adjacent.

reviews.summary: Te Pare delivers outstanding views and genuine Māori archaeological depth in a short, easy walk. Most visitors to Hahei focus on Cathedral Cove and miss this extraordinary headland entirely. The combination of pā earthworks, cultural interpretation, and panoramic coastal scenery is compelling.

pois.name: Hahei Heritage Walks
pois.short: A self-guided network of heritage walking tracks in the Hahei area connecting Ngāti Hei pā sites, scenic viewpoints, and cultural landmarks above Mercury Bay.
pois.commentary: The Hahei Heritage Walks form part of Te Ara o Hei (The Path of Hei), a network of connected cultural walking tracks celebrating the 700-year presence of Ngāti Hei in the Mercury Bay area. The walks connect Te Pare Point, Hereheretaura Pā, Grange Road viewpoints, and the coastal cliffs above Cathedral Cove, with interpretation panels telling the stories of the people and events associated with each location. Named for the ancestor Hei who arrived on the Arawa waka from Hawaiiki, the trail framework acknowledges that every significant headland and harbour feature in the area carries Māori names and oral traditions predating European contact by 400 or more years.

reviews.summary: The heritage walk network transforms a visit to Hahei from a beach trip into a genuinely layered cultural experience. The interpretation is well-written and deeply respectful of Ngāti Hei traditions. Best explored with the free map available from local information centres.

pois.name: Opera Point Historic Reserve (Whangapoua)
pois.short: A DOC-managed headland pā reserve on Whangapoua Harbour featuring well-preserved earthworks and Craig's historic sawmill site, 30 minutes north of Coromandel Town on SH25.
pois.commentary: Opera Point Historic Reserve sits on the northern head of Whangapoua Harbour, encompassing Opera Point Pā — a textbook example of a Māori headland fortification using the natural cliff face for defence. The earthwork terraces, ditches and banks on the landward approach are remarkably well-preserved and clearly legible in the landscape. The reserve also contains Craig's Sawmill, a remnant of the colonial kauri milling era that followed Māori occupation. The walk to the pā summit (approximately 30 minutes return) passes through coastal scrub with superb views over Whangapoua Harbour. DOC interpretation provides context on the site's occupants and defensive strategy. Off SH25 between Coromandel Town and Whitianga at 1738 Whangapoua Road.

reviews.summary: A quiet, rewarding heritage site that combines Māori archaeological interest with colonial timber history in a beautiful coastal setting. The pā earthworks are among the best preserved on the Coromandel Peninsula. Worth the short detour from SH25.

pois.name: New Chums Beach (Wainuiototo)
pois.short: Consistently ranked one of the world's most beautiful secluded beaches — a kilometre of pristine white sand backed by undeveloped native bush, accessible only on foot via Whangapoua.
pois.commentary: New Chums Beach was named one of the top 20 deserted beaches in the world by Britain's The Observer newspaper and has appeared on numerous international best-beach lists since. The beach's extraordinary quality stems from its complete lack of development — no road access, no facilities, and surrounding hills entirely covered in native coastal forest. Access requires a 25-minute walk from the northwest end of Whangapoua Beach, including a section over rocks (requires low tide) and through mature pōhutukawa-and-nikau forest. The beach retains the feel of genuine discovery even when a handful of other visitors are present. The name Wainuiototo refers to water features historically associated with the area in Māori oral tradition.

reviews.summary: New Chums consistently delivers on its extraordinary reputation — the walk feels like genuine exploration and the beach itself is magnificent in any weather. The lack of facilities is entirely appropriate; this is a place to be in, not served by. One of the Coromandel's true treasures.

pois.name: Cathedral Cove Walk (Mautohe / Te Whanganui-o-Hei)
pois.short: The iconic walk to New Zealand's most photographed natural arch — a carved white volcanic rock cathedral opening to a remote double beach within a DOC marine reserve.
pois.commentary: Cathedral Cove Marine Reserve (Te Whanganui-o-Hei) is one of New Zealand's top-ranked natural attractions, encompassing a 893-hectare coastal marine reserve and the land walk to the famous rock arch. The arch itself — a 10-metre-high volcanic rhyolite passage connecting two beaches — is the result of differential wave erosion over thousands of years. In summer, vehicle access to the car park is restricted; visitors must use the shuttle from Hahei or walk from the village. Te Mautohe Pā — a fortified site of Ngāti Hei — stands on the headland above the cove and can be visited on the walking track. The waters of the reserve are among the clearest on the New Zealand coast.

reviews.summary: Cathedral Cove more than earns its iconic status — the arch is genuinely breathtaking and the walk in is beautiful. The summer shuttle system has reduced crowding at the car park. Best visited in the early morning or late afternoon when the light is best and crowds thinnest.

pois.name: Coromandel Coastal Walkway
pois.short: A 10-km one-way DOC track traversing the rugged northern tip of the Coromandel Peninsula — dramatic coastal cliffs, pōhutukawa-draped headlands, and a beach accessible only on foot.
pois.commentary: The Coromandel Coastal Walkway runs from Fletcher Bay at the Peninsula's northern tip to Stony Bay in the east — 10 km of genuine coastal wilderness accessible only by water taxi, private vehicle on unsealed roads, or the walk itself. The track traverses open farmland, dense coastal scrub and cliff-edge sections, with views extending to the Hauraki Gulf islands, the Bay of Plenty, and on clear days to the Coromandel Range. DOC maintains the track as a backcountry experience — the isolation and scale of the landscape is profound. Pōhutukawa trees dominate the coastal fringes, particularly spectacular in December when they flower crimson. Shuttle services operate between Fletcher Bay and Coromandel Town.

reviews.summary: The Coromandel Coastal Walkway rewards the logistical effort required to reach the northern tip of the Peninsula. The coastal scenery is dramatic and the isolation is complete — this is the Coromandel as it was before roads arrived. Best walked in either direction, ideally with a water taxi for one end.

pois.name: Hauraki Rail Trail (Thames to Paeroa Section)
pois.short: A flat, scenic cycling and walking trail along the Firth of Thames shoreline through Ramsar-listed shorebird habitat and past gold-rush heritage towns.
pois.commentary: The Hauraki Rail Trail is one of 23 Ngā Haerenga Great Rides of New Zealand, following 197 km of mostly flat former rail corridors through the Hauraki Plains. The Thames–Paeroa section is the most scenically dramatic, tracing the Firth of Thames shoreline through the internationally significant Kaiaua wetlands (Ramsar-listed for migratory shorebird habitat) before turning inland to Paeroa past gold-rush heritage sites and the famous 'L&P' bottle. The trail is entirely off-road and suitable for all fitness levels on standard bikes. Gold mining history interpretation is excellent throughout. The Thames–Coromandel section passes close to the School of Mines and can be incorporated into a broader Coromandel heritage circuit.

reviews.summary: The Thames section of the Hauraki Rail Trail is the highlight of the network for most visitors — the combination of Firth shoreline scenery, excellent birdwatching, and gold-rush heritage interpretation is hard to beat at this level of physical effort. Suitable for families and casual cyclists.

pois.name: Thames Historical Museum
pois.short: A community-run museum documenting Thames from its pre-European Māori history through the gold rush era to the present — with particular strength in local Hauraki iwi heritage.
pois.commentary: The Thames Historical Museum holds collections across the full arc of Thames history: the pre-European occupation by Hauraki Māori (Ngāti Maru, Ngāti Tamaterā), the establishment of Thames as a gold-rush settlement from 1867, the rapid rise and fall of the colonial mining economy, and the post-gold transition to dairying and fishing. The museum's Māori collection includes carved taonga, personal effects, and oral history recordings that complement but do not duplicate the School of Mines' mineralogical focus. Staffed by community volunteers whose families have lived in Thames for generations. Located in the central heritage precinct near the School of Mines.

reviews.summary: The Thames Historical Museum is genuinely good for a community-run operation — the collections are well-presented, the volunteers are knowledgeable, and the Māori heritage content is better developed than in many comparable facilities. A worthwhile companion to the School of Mines next door.

pois.name: Pauanui Waterways and Tairua Cultural Landscape
pois.short: The Tairua-Pauanui harbour estuary system — a culturally significant Māori landscape of wetlands, sandbars, and coastal forest with excellent kayaking and a colonial-era township.
pois.commentary: The Tairua Harbour system is one of the finest estuarine environments on the Coromandel east coast, a shallow tidal lagoon flanked by sandy spits, wetlands and coastal forest. The area was an important seasonal resource zone for Hauraki Māori, who gathered kai moana (seafood), manu (birds), and freshwater resources here over many generations. The townships of Tairua and Pauanui sit either side of the harbour entrance, connected by a short water-taxi service. The cultural landscape interpretation at the Tairua visitor centre provides context on the harbour's Māori associations. Kayaking the estuary at high tide through mangroves and sandflats is the best way to experience the landscape.

reviews.summary: Tairua and the harbour estuary offer a quieter, more contemplative Coromandel experience than the packed beaches further north. The estuarine ecology and kayaking are superb, and the cultural landscape interpretation adds depth. Best explored by water.

pois.name: Waiau Kauri Grove Walk
pois.short: A DOC walk through one of the Coromandel Peninsula's finest surviving kauri groves — ancient trees up to 800 years old in a cathedral forest setting off SH25.
pois.commentary: The Waiau Kauri Grove preserves a remnant of the vast kauri forests that once covered most of the Coromandel Peninsula before logging removed them from the late 18th century onwards. The walk passes trees up to 800 years old and more than 2 metres in diameter, their silver-grey trunks rising like columns before the canopy branches emerge high above. Kauri were — and remain — spiritually significant taonga to Māori, associated with Tāne Mahuta (forest deity). DOC manages strict biosecurity protocols at the site to prevent the spread of kauri dieback disease (Phytophthora agathidicida), which threatens all remaining kauri in New Zealand. Boot cleaning stations must be used. The walk is 1 km return and requires approximately 45 minutes.

reviews.summary: The Waiau Kauri Grove delivers a genuinely impressive encounter with ancient trees — the scale of the larger specimens creates genuine awe. The DOC interpretation on kauri ecology and the dieback threat is well-presented. The biosecurity protocols are necessary and should be respected.

pois.name: Coromandel Coastal Walkway
pois.short: Stunning 10km coastal track between Stony Bay and Fletcher Bay.
pois.commentary: The Coromandel Coastal Walkway traverses the wild northern tip of the peninsula, weaving between farmland, regenerating bush, and dramatic clifftop sections with views to the Moehau Range and Hauraki Gulf islands. The 10-kilometre one-way track takes around three hours and is well-graded but exposed in sections. Most walkers arrange a shuttle or two-car setup. Highlights include Poley Bay's secluded beach, panoramic views from the high point above Sandy Bay, and the chance to spot kereru and tui in the bush sections. Carry water and sun protection as there is no shade on the farm sections.

reviews.summary: Walkers praise the rugged coastal scenery and lack of crowds. The shuttle logistics can be tricky but the track is well worth the effort.

pois.name: Whitianga Waterways & Marina
pois.short: Venice-style canal village with restaurants, galleries and water taxis.
pois.commentary: Whitianga Waterways is a modern canal-side development that has become a destination in its own right, with waterfront restaurants, art galleries, and boutique shops lining pedestrian boardwalks. Water taxis connect the waterways to Whitianga town centre and Ferry Landing across Mercury Bay. The area makes a pleasant base for exploring Cathedral Cove and Hot Water Beach, with several boat charter operators departing from the marina for fishing, diving, and scenic cruises. The Sunday morning vibe is particularly relaxed, with locals and visitors strolling the canals. Several excellent cafes serve fresh seafood with harbour views.

reviews.summary: A pleasant surprise for visitors expecting a quiet beach town. The waterfront dining is excellent and the water taxi adds a fun touch.

pois.name: New Chums Beach / Wainuiototo
pois.short: Pristine undeveloped beach consistently rated among the world's best.
pois.commentary: Accessible only on foot via a 30-minute bush track from Whangapoua Beach, New Chums is a kilometre-long crescent of golden sand backed entirely by native pohutukawa forest with no roads, buildings, or development of any kind. It has been rated among the world's top ten beaches by international travel publications and by the UK's Observer newspaper. The track crosses a headland with some rock scrambling at high tide, so check tide times before setting out. Swimming is generally safe in calm conditions but there are no lifeguards. The beach is part of a DOC reserve and visitors are asked to carry out all rubbish.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe it as paradise. The walk in keeps crowds small and the completely undeveloped setting feels like stepping back in time.

pois.name: Square Kauri / Kauritatahi
pois.short: Unique kauri tree with a naturally square trunk, accessed by short bush walk.
pois.commentary: Deep in the Coromandel forest, the Square Kauri is a botanical curiosity — a mature kauri tree whose trunk has grown in an almost perfectly square cross-section rather than the usual round shape. Scientists believe the unusual growth pattern may be caused by four symmetrically placed roots creating equal pressure on the trunk. The tree stands beside a short, well-formed loop track through beautiful native bush rich with tree ferns and nikau palms. The walk takes around 20 minutes return from the carpark on the 309 Road, which connects Coromandel Town to Whitianga. Combine with nearby Waiau Falls, another 309 Road attraction just a few minutes' drive away.

reviews.summary: A quick, easy stop that genuinely surprises people. The square shape is unmistakable and the surrounding bush is lovely.

pois.name: Cathedral Cove Sea Kayaking
pois.short: Paddle to Cathedral Cove, sea caves, and volcanic islands along the Coromandel coast.
pois.commentary: Sea kayaking to Cathedral Cove from Hahei is one of the best ways to experience the Coromandel's stunning coastline, paddling past volcanic rock formations, through sea caves, and landing on beaches inaccessible by foot. Guided tours typically visit Cathedral Cove's famous natural archway, explore sea caves and blowholes along the coast, and include snorkelling in the Te Whanganui-A-Hei Marine Reserve where fish are abundant and unafraid of swimmers. The volcanic geology creates a dramatic coastline of towering cliffs, stacks, and arches that are best appreciated from water level. Half-day tours depart from Hahei Beach and no prior kayaking experience is necessary. Morning departures offer calmer conditions.

reviews.summary: Hands-down the best way to see Cathedral Cove. The sea caves are incredible. Snorkelling in the marine reserve is a bonus.

pois.name: Coromandel Oyster Company
pois.short: Fresh Pacific oysters straight from Coromandel Harbour — eat them at the source.
pois.commentary: The Coromandel Oyster Company sells Pacific oysters direct from Coromandel Harbour, where the clean, nutrient-rich waters produce some of New Zealand's finest shellfish. You can buy oysters by the dozen, freshly shucked to eat on the spot or sealed to take away. The setting is industrial and no-frills — this is a working oyster operation, not a restaurant — which only adds to the authenticity. The oysters are plump, briny, and sweet, reflecting the harbour's pristine conditions. For a true Coromandel food experience, grab a dozen, add a squeeze of lemon, and eat them overlooking the harbour.

reviews.summary: The freshest oysters imaginable, eaten right where they're grown. No-frills perfection. Bring your own lemon.

pois.name: Purangi Estate Winery
pois.short: Rustic vineyard near Hahei with wood-fired pizza, wine tastings, and feijoa cider.
pois.commentary: Purangi Estate is a charming boutique winery near Hahei that embodies the relaxed Coromandel spirit. The vineyard produces small-batch wines and their famous feijoa cider, all available for tasting in a rustic open-air setting. Their wood-fired pizzas, made with local ingredients and served in the garden, have become legendary among Coromandel visitors. The atmosphere is utterly relaxed — picnic tables under the trees, vineyard views, and the sound of birds. It's the kind of place you drop into for a quick tasting and end up staying all afternoon. The feijoa cider is unique to Purangi and worth trying even if you're not a cider person.

reviews.summary: Wonderful rustic vineyard with outstanding wood-fired pizza. The feijoa cider is a must-try. Perfect lazy afternoon spot.

pois.name: Thames Saturday Market
pois.short: Weekly community market in Thames with local produce, arts, and crafts.
pois.commentary: The Thames Saturday Market at Grahamstown Market occupies the historic Grahamstown site every Saturday morning, bringing together local growers, artisan food producers, artists, and craftspeople. The market is a genuine community gathering rather than a tourist production — locals come for their weekly fruit and vegetables, bread, and coffee. You'll find Coromandel-made cheeses, preserves, baked goods, handcrafted jewellery, and local art alongside seasonal produce. The atmosphere is friendly and unhurried, and chatting with the stallholders is half the experience. Thames itself is worth exploring — the Victorian gold-mining town has good heritage buildings and antique shops.

reviews.summary: Authentic local market with genuine character. Great for picking up artisan foods and meeting locals. Worth an early start.

pois.name: Mercury Bay & Whitianga Harbour
pois.short: Sheltered harbour for kayaking, swimming, and exploring secluded beaches by water.
pois.commentary: Mercury Bay is the large, sheltered harbour that makes Whitianga the Coromandel's boating capital. The harbour offers calm water kayaking, paddleboarding, and swimming, with the ferry across to Ferry Landing providing access to beaches and walks on the eastern side. Shakespeare Cliff scenic reserve offers panoramic views over the bay. The area is historically significant as the landing place of the Polynesian explorer Kupe, and was named by Captain Cook who observed the Transit of Mercury here in 1769. Sea kayaking from Whitianga provides access to hidden coves, rock formations, and Marine Reserve areas that are impossible to reach by road.

reviews.summary: Beautiful sheltered harbour perfect for water activities. The kayak access to hidden coves is wonderful. Rich history too.

pois.name: Rapaura Watergardens
pois.short: Enchanting water gardens with cascades, native bush, and sculpture tucked into the forest.
pois.commentary: Rapaura Watergardens is one of the Coromandel's most enchanting hidden attractions — a series of ponds, waterfalls, and cascading streams threaded through 26 hectares of native bush and subtropical plantings. Walking paths wind past lily ponds, fern grottos, and small waterfalls, with sculptures and art installations placed throughout the garden. The overall effect is magical and slightly other-worldly. A swimming hole fed by a waterfall provides a refreshing dip on warm days. The gardens were created over decades by the landowners, who channeled natural springs and streams into the water features. It's the kind of place children find especially captivating.

reviews.summary: A hidden treasure. The water features and native bush create a magical atmosphere. Children love it. Allow more time than you think.

pois.name: Kauaeranga Valley & Pinnacles Walk
pois.short: Iconic overnight tramp through kauri dams to dramatic rock pinnacles above the forest canopy.
pois.commentary: The Kauaeranga Valley is the gateway to the Coromandel's most famous tramp — the Pinnacles Walk. The track climbs through historic kauri logging country, passing massive kauri dams built in the 1800s to flush logs downriver, before ascending steeply to the Pinnacles Hut. From the hut, a final scramble up ladders and rock ledges reaches the Pinnacles summit, where 360-degree views span the Coromandel Range to both coasts. Most trampers stay overnight at the hut to catch sunrise from the summit. The DOC visitor centre in the valley has excellent displays on the kauri logging history. The valley also offers shorter day walks suitable for families.

reviews.summary: One of the North Island's best overnight tramps. The sunrise from the Pinnacles is spectacular. Book the hut early.

pois.name: Hauraki Rail Trail — Thames Section
pois.short: Flat, scenic cycling along a historic railway line through the Hauraki Plains to Thames.
pois.commentary: The Hauraki Rail Trail is a flat, easy cycling trail that follows a historic railway line from the Hauraki Plains into Thames on the Coromandel Peninsula. The Thames section follows the coast along the Firth of Thames, with views across the water to Auckland. The flat gradient makes it accessible to casual cyclists and families. The trail passes through farmland, wetlands, and the historic goldfields around Paeroa and Waihi. Thames, at the trail's Coromandel end, offers heritage buildings, gold-mining history, and good cafes for refueling. Bike hire is available at multiple points along the trail.

reviews.summary: Easy, scenic cycling suitable for everyone. The coastal section into Thames is beautiful. Great heritage interest along the way.

pois.name: Mercury Bay Art Escape
pois.short: Community art events and open studios celebrating Whitianga's thriving creative scene.
pois.commentary: Mercury Bay Art Escape is an annual event (usually in January) where artists across the Whitianga and Mercury Bay area open their studios to the public, but the associated galleries and studios welcome visitors year-round. The Coromandel has long attracted artists — the quality of light, dramatic coastline, and relaxed lifestyle draw painters, sculptors, photographers, and craftspeople. The event typically features over 30 artists in locations from professional galleries to garden studios. Year-round, Whitianga has several permanent galleries showcasing local work. The event provides a wonderful way to explore the Mercury Bay area through an artistic lens, visiting studios in locations you'd never otherwise discover.

reviews.summary: Wonderful way to discover the area's creative community. The open-studio format is engaging. Good art in beautiful settings.

pois.name: Blackbeard's Smokehouse
pois.short: Artisan smokehouse in Whitianga producing exceptional smoked seafood and game meats.
pois.commentary: Blackbeard's Smokehouse in Whitianga is one of the Coromandel's culinary treasures — a small artisan operation producing exceptional hot-smoked fish, mussels, and game meats using traditional methods. The smokehouse processes locally caught fish and Coromandel green-lipped mussels, smoking them over native wood chips to produce intensely flavoured products. You can buy directly from the smokehouse and eat at their small counter or take away for picnics. The smoked kahawai and smoked mussel are local favourites. It's the kind of authentic, producer-direct food experience that captures the Coromandel's connection to the sea.

reviews.summary: Outstanding smoked seafood — you can taste the quality of local ingredients. Buy direct for the best prices. Perfect picnic provisions.

pois.name: Colville General Store & Bay
pois.short: NZ's northernmost general store co-op in a remote, alternative-lifestyle community.
pois.commentary: Colville is the last settlement before the wild northern tip of the Coromandel Peninsula, and its cooperative general store is a local institution. Run as a community cooperative since 1978, the store stocks everything from organic groceries to hardware, serving the area's alternative-lifestyle community of artists, organic farmers, and bush dwellers. The cafe serves excellent food with a distinctly counter-cultural flavour. Beyond Colville, the road turns to gravel and leads to remote beaches and DOC campsites at Port Jackson and Fletcher Bay. Colville represents the Coromandel's free-spirited character at its most authentic — this is not a tourist destination but a real community at the end of the road.

reviews.summary: Authentic alternative community with a great co-op store and cafe. The gateway to the wild northern Coromandel. Real character.

pois.name: Fletcher Bay to Stingray Bay Walk
pois.short: Remote coastal walk at the very tip of the Coromandel Peninsula — wild and uncrowded.
pois.commentary: The walk from Fletcher Bay to Stingray Bay traverses the remote northern tip of the Coromandel Peninsula through stunning coastal scenery. The 3-hour track follows farmland and bush along the coast with views across to Great Barrier Island and the Mercury Islands. Fletcher Bay itself is a beautiful DOC campsite at the end of the road — literally the last accessible point on the peninsula. The walk to Stingray Bay crosses headlands and descends to a sheltered bay where stingrays are sometimes seen in the shallows. The remoteness and lack of visitors give this walk a genuine wilderness quality rare on the Coromandel. The unsealed road to Fletcher Bay is part of the adventure.

reviews.summary: Beautifully remote walking at the end of the peninsula. Few other walkers. The DOC campsite is peaceful. A real get-away-from-it-all experience.

pois.name: Thames Goldmine & Stamper Battery
pois.short: Underground mine tour and working stamper battery from the 1860s Thames gold rush.
pois.commentary: The Thames Goldmine Experience offers underground mine tours and demonstrates a working stamper battery from the 1868 Thames gold rush — at its peak, the richest goldfield in New Zealand. The guided mine tour takes visitors into the tunnels where quartz gold was extracted, while the surface stamper battery — massive machines that crushed gold-bearing quartz — is demonstrated in deafening action. Thames was once larger than Auckland during the gold rush, and the mine remains bring that era to vivid life. The experience provides context for the gold-mining heritage visible throughout the Coromandel Peninsula. Hard hats, torches, and waterproofs are provided.

reviews.summary: Fascinating underground experience. The stamper battery is impressively loud. Good guides bring the gold rush alive.

pois.name: Whangamata Beach & Surf
pois.short: Classic NZ surf beach town with consistent waves, the famous Williamson Park bar break, and holiday vibes.
pois.commentary: Whangamata is the Coromandel's premier surf town — a laid-back beach settlement with excellent and consistent surf breaks. The main beach offers gentle waves suitable for learners, while the famous Williamson Park bar break at the harbour entrance produces powerful, barrel-shaped waves that attract experienced surfers from around the country. The town has a classic New Zealand beach-holiday character — ice cream shops, fish and chips, surf hire, and a relaxed social scene. The harbour offers calmer water for kayaking, paddleboarding, and fishing. The Wentworth Valley walk nearby leads to a waterfall in native bush. Whangamata fills to bursting over Christmas and New Year but is peaceful in the shoulder seasons.

reviews.summary: Classic NZ surf beach with great waves. The bar break is world-class for experienced surfers. Lovely holiday town character.

pois.name: The Pinnacles / Kauaeranga Kauri Trail
pois.short: Dramatic overnight hike to rocky pinnacles with panoramic views.
pois.commentary: The Pinnacles Track is one of the Coromandel's most popular overnight hikes, climbing through regenerating kauri forest in the Kauaeranga Valley to a spectacular cluster of rocky pinnacles at 759m. The track passes old kauri dams and logging relics, remnants of the extensive milling that stripped these forests in the 19th century. The DOC-managed Pinnacles Hut sleeps 80 and is well-equipped, making it one of NZ's more comfortable backcountry overnights. The final scramble to the summit involves ladders and chain handholds, rewarding with 360-degree views from the Coromandel Range to both coasts. It can be done as a long day walk (7-8 hours return) or a more relaxed overnight. Book hut passes through DOC.

reviews.summary: The summit views are breathtaking, especially at sunrise for overnight visitors. The kauri dam heritage adds interest. The hut is comfortable for a backcountry experience.

pois.name: Gemstone Bay Snorkelling Trail
pois.short: Underwater snorkelling trail through a marine reserve with abundant fish life.
pois.commentary: Gemstone Bay is a small, sheltered cove near Cathedral Cove with an underwater snorkelling trail — a series of stainless steel posts on the seabed guiding snorkellers through the highlights of the Te Whanganui-A-Hei Marine Reserve. The reserve's protected waters teem with fish species including snapper, blue maomao, goatfish, and occasionally eagle rays. The trail takes about 30-45 minutes and is suitable for confident swimmers. Bring your own gear or hire from Hahei Beach. The bay is accessed via a short walk from the Cathedral Cove car park, and being in the marine reserve means no fishing or collecting is permitted. Water clarity is best on calm days. A quieter alternative to nearby Cathedral Cove.

reviews.summary: Snorkellers love the clear water and abundant marine life. The underwater trail posts are helpful for navigation. Much quieter than Cathedral Cove nearby.

pois.name: The Lost Spring
pois.short: Geothermal hot pools in a lush tropical garden setting in Whitianga.
pois.commentary: The Lost Spring in Whitianga is a unique day spa built around naturally heated geothermal water discovered in 2004 after a 7-year search for hot water beneath the town. The complex features multiple hot pools of varying temperatures, set among lush tropical gardens with palm trees, waterfalls, and Balinese-inspired architecture. The water emerges at 51°C from 670 metres below ground and is rich in minerals. The adults-only pools offer a serene, luxurious experience, while a family pool caters to visitors with children. On-site dining serves cocktails and café food poolside. Day passes are limited to maintain a peaceful atmosphere, so booking is essential, particularly in summer. A hidden gem of the Coromandel.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe it as an oasis. The tropical gardens and warm mineral pools create a blissful experience. Booking ahead is essential as it's often full.

pois.name: Hot Water Brewing Company
pois.short: Award-winning craft brewery on a converted farm near Hot Water Beach.
pois.commentary: The Hot Water Brewing Company is a craft brewery set on a converted farm in Whenuakite, just minutes from the famous Hot Water Beach. The brewery produces a range of award-winning beers using local water and NZ hops, with styles ranging from easy-drinking lagers to bold IPAs. The taproom has a relaxed rural atmosphere with outdoor seating overlooking the farm. Tasting paddles allow visitors to sample the full range. The brewery often hosts food trucks and live music on weekends in summer. It's a popular post-beach stop for visitors to Hot Water Beach and Cathedral Cove, offering a refreshing reward after a day of digging hot pools in the sand.

reviews.summary: Beer lovers enjoy the quality brews and the relaxed farm setting. Great stop after a day at Hot Water Beach. The tasting paddles offer good variety.

pois.name: The Lost Spring, Whitianga
pois.short: Geothermal hot pools in a lush tropical garden setting — one of the Coromandel's most luxurious relaxation experiences.
pois.commentary: The Lost Spring draws naturally heated water from deep underground — discovered by accident during a property survey — and channels it through a series of beautifully designed pools set among tropical gardens, waterfalls and palm trees. The water emerges at 58°C and is cooled to bathing temperature in different pools, each with its own character. There's a swim-up bar serving cocktails and the day spa offers treatments using natural products. It feels like a Balinese retreat transplanted to the Coromandel. Booking is essential, especially in summer and school holidays, as numbers are limited to maintain the tranquil atmosphere.

reviews.summary: A beautiful and relaxing experience. The tropical setting is stunning. The swim-up bar is a highlight. Some find prices steep but consider it worth it. Book well in advance.

pois.name: Orokawa Bay Coastal Walk
pois.short: A 45-minute coastal walk from Waihi Beach to a secluded bay — pristine sand, clear water, and blissful solitude.
pois.commentary: Orokawa Bay is one of those places that rewards the effort of getting there. The 45-minute walk from the northern end of Waihi Beach crosses farmland and passes through coastal bush before dropping down to a perfect crescent of golden sand. The bay is sheltered and the swimming is excellent in calm conditions. There are no facilities — bring everything you need — and the bay is usually shared with only a handful of other walkers. The return walk is the same track, so time your visit to allow for a swim and lunch on the sand before heading back.

reviews.summary: A beautiful hidden beach worth every step of the walk. The swimming is excellent. No facilities so come prepared. Can be busy on summer weekends but quieter midweek.

pois.name: Stingray Bay / Te Whanganui-a-Hei
pois.short: A tranquil, sheltered beach often overlooked by visitors heading to Cathedral Cove — perfect for quiet swimming and snorkelling.
pois.commentary: Stingray Bay sits just north of Cathedral Cove but receives a fraction of the visitors. The beach is smaller and more intimate, with clear water sheltered by rocky headlands that make it ideal for swimming and snorkelling. It falls within the Te Whanganui-a-Hei Marine Reserve, so underwater life is abundant — expect to see snapper, blue maomao, and colourful reef fish. Access is via the same Cathedral Cove walking track, with a short spur branching off to Stingray Bay. Most people walk right past the turnoff without noticing. Their loss is your gain.

reviews.summary: A beautiful alternative to crowded Cathedral Cove. The snorkelling is excellent. Much quieter than its famous neighbour. Some note the access path is steep. Marine life is abundant.

pois.name: Kauri Block Track, Coromandel Town
pois.short: A short, steep walk through regenerating kauri forest to a panoramic lookout over Coromandel Harbour and the Hauraki Gulf.
pois.commentary: The Kauri Block Track in Coromandel Town is a compact gem — a 30-minute climb through regenerating kauri forest to a lookout with panoramic views over the harbour, township and Hauraki Gulf islands. The track passes through bush that was heavily logged in the 19th century but is now growing back strongly, with young kauri alongside mature podocarps and tree ferns. The viewpoint is particularly rewarding at sunset when the harbour turns gold. It's a less crowded, more accessible alternative to the longer kauri walks on the peninsula.

reviews.summary: Great views for minimal effort. The regenerating kauri forest is interesting. Best at sunset. Some find the track steep but it's short. A good option if you're staying in Coromandel Town.

pois.name: Lonely Bay
pois.short: A small, intimate beach framed by towering pōhutukawa trees and rocky cliffs — one of the Coromandel's loveliest swimming spots.
pois.commentary: Lonely Bay lives up to its name — a small crescent of golden sand sheltered by headlands and shaded by ancient pōhutukawa trees, accessed via a short walk from Cooks Beach or by car along a narrow road. The swimming is safe and the water clear, with rocky edges perfect for snorkelling. In December and January the pōhutukawa bloom crimson red, creating one of the most photogenic beach scenes on the peninsula. Despite its proximity to popular Cooks Beach and Cathedral Cove, Lonely Bay is often quiet, especially on weekday mornings. A towel, a book, and a few hours here is time well spent.

reviews.summary: A beautiful, intimate beach. The pōhutukawa in bloom are spectacular. Swimming is safe and enjoyable. Can get busy on summer weekends but midweek it's peaceful.

pois.name: Ōpito Bay Snorkelling & Rock Pools
pois.short: A sheltered bay with excellent snorkelling over reef and rock pools — clear water and abundant marine life.
pois.commentary: Ōpito Bay is a small, sheltered cove on the eastern Coromandel coast that offers some of the best snorkelling on the peninsula. The rocky headlands at either end of the bay create a natural aquarium of clear water over reef, kelp and sand, teeming with small fish, starfish, anemones and occasional octopus. At low tide, the rock pools are fascinating for children. The beach is backed by pōhutukawa trees and the vibe is relaxed and local. Access is via a narrow road from Kuaotunu — check tides for best snorkelling conditions. A snorkel and mask are all you need.

reviews.summary: Excellent snorkelling in clear water. The rock pools are great for kids. A beautiful, quiet beach. Access road is narrow. Best at mid to low tide.

pois.name: The Pinnacles Sunrise Hike (Kauaeranga Valley)
pois.short: An overnight hike to the Pinnacles summit for sunrise over the Coromandel — 360-degree views from the volcanic spine of the peninsula.
pois.commentary: The Pinnacles Track in the Kauaeranga Valley climbs through regenerating kauri forest to the rocky Pinnacles summit at 759 metres. The experience is at its best as an overnight trip — stay at the DOC Pinnacles Hut (book ahead) and climb to the summit for sunrise, when the 360-degree views over the Coromandel Peninsula, Hauraki Gulf and Bay of Plenty are bathed in golden light. The track passes through forest rich in history — kauri logging heritage is visible in old dams, tramways and the remains of a vast timber industry. The summit scramble involves fixed chains on exposed rock but is manageable for most. A rite of passage for Auckland and Waikato trampers.

reviews.summary: The sunrise from the summit is unforgettable. The hut is well-maintained. The forest walk is beautiful. Book the hut well ahead. The final summit scramble is steep but exciting.

pois.name: Ōpoutere Beach & Wharekawa Estuary
pois.short: Remote undeveloped beach with a pristine estuary and endangered dotterel nesting.
pois.commentary: Ōpoutere Beach requires a forest walk to access, keeping it beautifully uncrowded. The Wharekawa Estuary and sandspit are nesting habitat for endangered New Zealand dotterels and variable oystercatchers, making it both a beach paradise and important conservation site.

reviews.summary: Wonderfully remote — the walk through the forest makes it feel like a discovery.

pois.name: Kauri Block Walk
pois.short: Historic walk to a Māori pā site with 360-degree views over Coromandel.
pois.commentary: The Kauri Block Walk is a 3.1km return trail passing through regenerating kauri forest to a historic Māori pā site. From the summit, 360-degree views stretch over Coromandel Town, the harbour, and offshore islands — a cultural and scenic walk combined.

reviews.summary: Great short walk with cultural interest and superb views.

pois.name: Orokawa Bay Walk
pois.short: Coastal walk from Waihi Beach to a secluded golden beach backed by native bush.
pois.commentary: A 45-minute walk along the clifftops from Waihi Beach leads to Orokawa Bay, a pristine golden beach backed by regenerating native bush. With no road access, the bay feels truly remote and is perfect for swimming, picnicking, or simply enjoying the solitude.

reviews.summary: Absolutely stunning hidden beach — worth every step of the walk.

pois.name: Te Pare Historic Reserve — Hahei
pois.short: Historic Māori pā site on a dramatic headland with sweeping ocean views.
pois.commentary: At the southern end of Hahei Beach, Te Pare Historic Reserve occupies a dramatic headland that once held a fortified Māori pā. The short walk offers spectacular ocean views including a natural blowhole, and interpretive panels explain the site's significance — often missed by visitors heading straight to Cathedral Cove.

reviews.summary: Often overlooked for Cathedral Cove but equally impressive views.

pois.name: Whenuakura (Donut Island)
pois.short: Paddle through a rock tunnel into a collapsed volcanic crater island lagoon.
pois.commentary: Whenuakura Island, known as Donut Island, is a volcanic ring with a hidden lagoon accessible only through a rock tunnel at low tide. Kayakers paddle through the narrow passage to emerge in a sheltered crater lagoon — one of the Coromandel's most extraordinary natural features.

reviews.summary: Jaw-dropping — paddling into the hidden lagoon is unforgettable.

pois.name: Broken Hills Gold Mining Walk
pois.short: Bush walk through historic gold mining tunnels and ruins in the Coromandel Ranges.
pois.commentary: The Broken Hills walkway follows old mining tracks through dense bush to a series of hand-hewn mining tunnels from the 1890s gold rush. Bring a torch to explore the tunnels, and look for glowworms inside — a unique blend of heritage and nature.

reviews.summary: Great heritage walk — the tunnels with glowworms are amazing.

pois.name: Castle Rock Track — Whitianga
pois.short: Bush walk to a dramatic rock pinnacle with views across the Mercury Islands.
pois.commentary: Castle Rock rises above the Coromandel bush as a dramatic volcanic pinnacle. The track climbs through dense forest to the summit ridge where panoramic views extend across Mercury Bay to the offshore islands. It's a more challenging walk that rewards with spectacular scenery.

reviews.summary: Stunning views from the top — worth the climb.

pois.name: Wentworth Valley Falls
pois.short: A DOC walk from Wentworth Valley campground through forest and stream crossings to a high waterfall viewpoint.
pois.commentary: Wentworth Valley Falls is a useful reminder that the Coromandel is not only beaches and headlands. From the campground at the end of Wentworth Valley Road, the track follows the Wentworth River through Coromandel Forest Park, crossing bridged streams before climbing to a view of the falls. The route is usually straightforward, but DOC notes storm-damaged, uneven and slippery sections, so it belongs in the sturdy-shoes category rather than the casual-sandal one. Add it to a Whangamatā stay when you want cool bush, gold-country valley atmosphere and the sound of water instead of surf.

reviews.summary: Visitors like the easy forest approach and waterfall payoff; recent weather and slippery sections are the recurring practical notes.

pois.name: Waiau Falls and Kauri Grove
pois.short: Two easy 309 Road stops combining a swimming waterfall and a short walk among young kauri.
pois.commentary: Waiau Falls and the nearby kauri grove are classic Coromandel side-road rewards. One stop gives you water dropping into a rocky pool; the other leads into the quiet vertical presence of kauri, with boot-cleaning care part of the visit.

pois.name: Pauanui and Paku View
pois.short: A beach-town pause opposite Tairua, with harbour views back to Paku and the Coromandel ranges.
pois.commentary: Pauanui is planned, tidy and holiday-house polished, but its geography is still the star. Stand by the harbour or beach and Paku rises across the water, turning the Tairua entrance into a natural amphitheatre of sand, tide and volcanic rock.

pois.name: Kauaeranga Model Dam Walk
pois.short: A short Kauaeranga Valley walk interpreting the timber dams once used to drive kauri logs downstream.
pois.commentary: The Kauaeranga model dam helps make an almost unbelievable industry legible. Kauri logs were once held back behind bush dams and released in violent floods; this walk gives that history a physical shape while the forest slowly repairs itself around the story.

pois.name: Whangamatā Estuary
pois.short: A sheltered estuary behind Whangamatā’s surf beach, good for paddling, birdlife and gentle water time.
pois.commentary: Whangamatā is famous for surf, but the estuary tells the quieter half of the town’s water story. At the right tide it becomes a calm paddling world of sandbanks, channels and shorebirds, especially welcome when the ocean side is too rough.

pois.name: Kōpū Bridge and Rail Trail Gateway
pois.short: The southern Thames gateway to rail-trail riding, with the old Kōpū bridge marking a shift from highway to history.
pois.commentary: Kōpū is easy to pass through, but it quietly frames the Coromandel’s rail-trail story. The old bridge and flat Hauraki Plains tracks mark the change from car travel to slower wheels, where rivers, wetlands and gold towns begin to connect at cycling speed.

pois.name: Te Kouma Harbour
pois.short: Te Kouma Harbour is a sheltered western Coromandel inlet where boats, hills and calm water make a gentle counterpoint to the peninsula’s surf beaches.

pois.short: Matatoki Cheese Barn is a farm-food stop near Thames where tasting, animals and casual meals make the Hauraki Plains feel deliciously close at hand.

pois.name: Square Kauri Tree
pois.short: A short Coromandel Forest Park walk to an unusually square-trunked kauri above the 309 Road.
pois.commentary: The Square Kauri Tree is a small stop with a memorable shape. A short climb leads to an old kauri whose trunk seems almost deliberately carved by nature, making it a useful pause on the 309 Road and a reminder to respect kauri hygiene at every forest entrance.

pois.name: Broken Hills Battery Walk
pois.short: A short Broken Hills walk through Coromandel gold-mining relics, forest and river-country remains.
pois.commentary: Broken Hills Battery Walk lets the Coromandel gold story sit inside the bush rather than behind glass. Old workings and battery remains show how much effort was dragged into this valley, while the Tairua River and regenerating forest keep softening the edges.

pois.short: A named peak that helps fill the inland skyline of Coromandel Peninsula.
pois.commentary: Maumaupaki / Camels Back / Te Maungamaupaki helps mark the high country and skyline around Coromandel Peninsula. Peaks are not all visitor-ready tracks, so the value here is orientation first: a named point that explains what you are seeing from roads, lookouts and nearby walks.
